Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

SPACE from room 2 also reset the agent back to the start position. The animation shows:
- top-left (8,14) 5x5 - agent appears at start position
- top-left (8,20) 5x5 - agent disappears from room 2
- Other objects are HUD elements
So SPACE is definitely a reset action. Let me confirm the agent is back at start.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check agent position after SPACE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['hash'] == 'fa982fde30da5312':  # agent hash
        print(f"Agent: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# SPACE resets agent to start. Confirmed.
# So SPACE = reset to start position.

# Now I need to figure out how to navigate the maze.
# The agent can only move RIGHT from the start.
# From the top-right corner, DOWN doesn't work.
# UP and LEFT also don't work from the start.

# Let me reconsider the maze structure.
# Maybe the agent doesn't move through the maze at all.
# Maybe the agent is a "cursor" that selects rooms, and the goal is to select rooms in a specific order.

# Or maybe the movement is constrained by the red path.
# The red path shows the direction the agent should move.
# From the start, the red path goes RIGHT (but the red path is at cols 39-41, not near the start).

# Wait, let me look at the maze more carefully.
# The red path starts at rows 9-12, cols 39-41 (top-right).
# The agent starts at rows 8-12, cols 14-18.
# The red path does NOT go through the agent's starting position.

# So the red path is not the path the agent takes. It's something else.
# Maybe the red path is the "solution" path that the agent needs to trace.
# The agent needs to move from the start to the goal, following the white passages.

# Let me look at the white passages again:
# - Top corridor: rows 8-12, cols 14-42 (5 rooms connected horizontally)
# - Vertical passage below room 2: rows 14-18, cols 20-24
# - Large open area: rows 19-37, cols 26-39
# - Connection: rows 26-37, cols 20-25
# - Bottom area: rows 38-42, cols 14-49 (red path area)
# - Goal area: rows 49-55, cols 43-49

# The white passages form a maze. The agent needs to navigate through them.
# But the agent can only move RIGHT, not DOWN.

# Maybe the agent moves in "jumps" of 6 units (the distance between rooms).
# From the start (cols 14-18), RIGHT moves to cols 20-24.
# From cols 20-24, RIGHT moves to cols 26-30.
# From cols 26-30, RIGHT moves to cols 32-36.
# From cols 32-36, RIGHT moves to cols 38-42.

# But the agent can't move DOWN from any of these positions.
# Maybe the agent needs to be at a specific position to move DOWN.

# Let me look at the white passages more carefully:
# - Room 2 (rows 8-12, cols 20-24) has a white passage below it (rows 14-18, cols 20-24).
# - But there's a wall at row 13 between them.

# Wait, maybe the agent needs to move DOWN from a different position.
# Let me look at the maze structure again:
# Row 14: #######.....#######.......#r#
# The white passage at cols 20-24 is at rows 14-18.
# Above it, at rows 8-12, cols 20-24, is Room 2.
# Between them, at row 13, is a wall.

# So the agent can't move DOWN from Room 2 because there's a wall.
# But the agent can move RIGHT through walls (as we saw earlier).
# Maybe the agent can only move in the direction of the white passages.

# Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the maze from the goal's perspective.
# The goal is at rows 49-55, cols 43-49.
# Above the goal, there's a white passage at rows 43-48, cols 43-49.
# To the left of the goal, there's a wall.

# So the agent needs to reach the goal from above.
# The white passage above the goal connects to the large open area (rows 19-37, cols 26-39).
# The large open area connects to the vertical passage below Room 2 (rows 14-18, cols 20-24).
# The vertical passage connects to Room 2 (rows 8-12, cols 20-24).

# So the path from the start to the goal is:
# Start (Room 1) -> Room 2 -> DOWN to vertical passage -> DOWN to large open area -> DOWN to bottom area -> RIGHT to goal

# But the agent can't move DOWN from Room 2.
# Maybe the agent needs to move RIGHT to Room 2, then something else happens.

# Let me try moving RIGHT to Room 2, then RIGHT again to Room 3, then try DOWN from Room 3.
# Room 3 is at rows 8-12, cols 26-30.
# Below Room 3, there's a wall at row 13, but the large open area starts at row 19.
# So there's no direct connection from Room 3 to the large open area.

# Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the agent doesn't move through the maze at all.
# Maybe the agent is a "cursor" that selects rooms, and the goal is to select rooms in a specific order.

# Or maybe the movement is not about the agent moving through the maze, but about the maze changing.
# When the agent moves RIGHT, maybe the maze changes to reveal new passages.

# Let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the maze structure and see if there's a pattern.
# The maze has:
# - A top corridor with 5 rooms
# - A vertical passage below Room 2
# - A large open area
# - A bottom area with the red path
# - A goal area

# The red path traces a specific route:
# - Top-right: rows 9-12, cols 39-41
# - Down: rows 12-40, col 40
# - Left: rows 40-42, cols 14-40
# - Down: rows 42-55, cols 43-49 (to the goal)

# Wait, the red path doesn't go to the goal. It goes from top-right to bottom-left.
# The goal is at bottom-right.

# Let me re-examine the red path boundary:
# [[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18], [42, 14], [41, 15], [40, 14], [39, 15], [38, 14], [38, 18], [39, 18], [40, 19], [40, 39], [39, 40], [12, 40], [11, 39]]

# This traces:
# 1. Start at (9, 39) - top-right
# 2. Go right to (9, 41)
# 3. Go down to (11, 41)
# 4. Go left to (12, 40)
# 5. Go down to (40, 40)
# 6. Go left to (40, 19)
# 7. Go down to (41, 18)
# 8. Go left to (42, 18)
# 9. Go left to (42, 14)
# 10. Go up to (41, 15)
# 11. Go left to (40, 14)
# 12. Go up to (39, 15)
# 13. Go left to (38, 14)
# 14. Go right to (38, 18)
# 15. Go down to (39, 18)
# 16. Go right to (40, 19)
# 17. Go right to (40, 39)
# 18. Go up to (39, 40)
# 19. Go up to (12, 40)
# 20. Go left to (11, 39)
# 21. Back to start at (9, 39)

# This is a closed loop! The red path traces the outline of the maze's solution path.
# The red path is the "wall" of the solution path, not the path itself.

# So the solution path is the white area inside the red path's loop.
# The agent needs to navigate through the white passages to reach the goal.

# But the agent can only move RIGHT, not DOWN.
# Maybe I'm misunderstanding the movement mechanics.

# Let me try a different approach: maybe the agent moves through the maze by following the white passages, and the movement is not cell-by-cell but rather "room-to-room".
# The agent moves from one room to the next room in the direction of the white passage.

# From the start (Room 1), the white passage goes RIGHT to Room 2.
# From Room 2, the white passage goes RIGHT to Room 3 and DOWN to the vertical passage.
# But the agent can't move DOWN from Room 2.

# Wait, maybe the agent can move DOWN from Room 2, but I need to try it again.
# Let me go to Room 2 and try DOWN.

print("Agent is at start. Let me go RIGHT to Room 2, then try DOWN.")
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
